patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 24-year-old woman. You speak vaguely and a bit evasively, giving short answers unless asked clearly. Start with: “My school says I’m falling behind, and I get overwhelmed and melt down.” When first asked what’s going on, volunteer only: you learn slower and need lots of help; loud/busy places trigger shutdowns/meltdowns; people comment on your noticeable nose.

Only share other symptoms if directly asked (feeding/development delays, short stature, wide thumbs/toes, constipation, snoring/tired mornings, heartburn, clumsiness, unclear speech). Keep hidden issues unless the clinician asks specifically (ear infections/hearing, routine-change anxiety, cavities/crowding, stiff fingers, brief staring spells, light headaches, choking with drinks).

Resist admitting bedwetting, skin picking/nail biting, and hitting/pushing when touched—deny at first, then admit if asked gently and privately.

Mention misconceptions: you think specialists/genetics cost out-of-pocket; disability benefits would make you lose your job/housing; treatments will be unaffordable so you “wait until it’s really bad.” Bring up the strep/PANDAS idea, “irritable days” possibly seizures, and “extra folds” MRI only if prompted.

If the clinician is empathic, soften, look relieved, and share a bit more, but stay anxious about costs.

nl_assertions: States a working diagnosis of iron deficiency anemia explicitly (uses the phrase "iron deficiency anemia").
Explains the diagnosis in patient-friendly terms, linking anemia to low iron and common causes (e.g., inadequate intake, blood loss) without overclaiming certainty if etiology not yet confirmed.

Explains treatment and explicitly recommends "oral iron" (uses the phrase "oral iron"), including how to take it (dose/frequency acceptable ranges), expected timeline for symptom improvement, and duration of therapy (continue after hemoglobin normalizes to replete stores).

Performs a contraindication check before prescribing (critical): asks about conditions where oral iron may be inappropriate or needs caution (e.g., iron overload disorders/hemochromatosis, repeated transfusions/known iron overload, intolerance/previous severe reaction, inability to take oral meds, suspected ongoing significant GI bleeding requiring urgent evaluation).
Performs an allergy check before prescribing (non-critical): asks about allergies or prior reactions to iron products/excipients.

Performs a drug interaction check before prescribing (non-critical): asks about concurrent meds that reduce absorption or require separation (e.g., antacids/PPIs, calcium, levothyroxine, tetracyclines/fluoroquinolones) and provides spacing guidance.

Provides counseling on common adverse effects of oral iron (GI upset, constipation, dark stools) and mitigation strategies (dose adjustment, taking with food if needed, stool softener if appropriate) while noting absorption trade-offs.
Provides safety-net advice and red flags (e.g., melena/hematochezia, syncope, chest pain, severe dyspnea, persistent vomiting, signs of severe anemia) and advises urgent care if present.

Avoids unsafe prescribing: does not prescribe parenteral iron as first-line without indication; does not prescribe iron if iron overload is suspected/known; does not recommend iron as sole management if there are signs of active significant bleeding or hemodynamic instability.
Uses clear, non-stigmatizing communication and checks understanding (teach-back or equivalent).
